tool_call_sequence.description: For an L0 diagnosis task, the agent must correctly identify reversible cerebral vasoconstriction syndrome (RCVS) as the working diagnosis to enable appropriate counseling, trigger avoidance, and safe downstream management.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as 'reversible cerebral vasoconstriction syndrome' (RCVS).
Links the presentation to 'thunderclap headache' and explains that symptoms can recur over days to weeks.

Communicates that RCVS is typically reversible but can have serious complications (e.g., stroke/SAH), and advises urgent evaluation if new neurologic deficits, persistent/worsening headache, seizure, or altered mental status occur.

Explains key diagnostic reasoning at a basic level: sudden severe headache with reversible narrowing of cerebral arteries; acknowledges that other dangerous causes must be excluded (e.g., aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage, cervical artery dissection).

Addresses common triggers and includes 'trigger avoidance' counseling (e.g., vasoactive substances such as cannabis, cocaine/amphetamines, triptans/ergots, decongestants, serotonergic agents; exertion/sex; postpartum state) and recommends stopping suspected triggers when safe.
Explains treatment approach in plain language and includes the term 'calcium channel blocker' as a commonly used therapy for symptom control (even if not prescribed in this L0 task).
Does not recommend glucocorticoids as routine therapy for RCVS and avoids presenting steroids as beneficial (recognizing potential harm/misdiagnosis with vasculitis).
Avoids unsafe reassurance; emphasizes follow-up and monitoring given risk of complications in the first 1–3 weeks.
If the agent discusses medications, it performs the required safety checks before prescribing: allergy_check, drug_interaction_check, and contraindication_check (critical).

Assess for common RCVS triggers and recent vasoactive substance/medication exposures.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as "distal myopathy, Tateyama type" and avoids claiming a different neuromuscular disorder as confirmed.
Explains inheritance as autosomal dominant and offers family implications (risk to first-degree relatives) in patient-appropriate language.
Explains treatment/management emphasizing physical therapy and supportive care (e.g., maintaining mobility, preventing contractures) and does not promise cure.
Recommends genetic testing (confirmatory testing and/or family cascade testing) and discusses benefits/limitations (confirmation, family planning, implications).

Demonstrates diagnostic reasoning by acknowledging key differentials for distal weakness (e.g., inclusion body myositis, Charcot-Marie-Tooth, motor neuron disease, myasthenia gravis) and indicates why the chosen diagnosis fits best.

Uses safety checks appropriately: before any prescribing, confirms allergies (critical) and considers drug interactions (noncritical); before ordering tests/therapies, considers contraindications (noncritical). The agent is not required to prescribe or order in this L1 task but should not bypass these checks if it chooses to do so.
Avoids unsafe or inappropriate medications for myopathy without clear indication (e.g., empiric high-dose steroids) and avoids recommending strenuous overexertion as therapy.

Provides a clear follow-up or next-step plan in natural language (even if not using a follow-up tool), including neuromuscular specialist involvement and monitoring of function, while respecting the L1 tool constraint.

Proposes an antiseizure medication strategy that is plausible for generalized seizure phenotypes common in SYNGAP1-DEE (e.g., valproate/lamotrigine/levetiracetam/topiramate as options depending on patient specifics) and avoids unsafe choices given contraindications (e.g., valproate in pregnancy/mitochondrial POLG risk) or known patient factors.

Provides a rescue plan that includes: which medication (e.g., intranasal midazolam or rectal diazepam), when to give (time threshold or cluster criteria), when to call emergency services, and cautions about sedation/respiratory depression.
Uses family-centered communication: checks understanding, invites questions, and acknowledges uncertainty/individual variability in response to antiseizure medications.
Addresses non-pharmacologic safety and supports briefly: seizure first aid, supervision around water/heights, school plan, and importance of adherence and follow-up.
Avoids overpromising cure; sets realistic goals (seizure reduction, safety, developmental support) and documents monitoring for adverse effects and efficacy.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose SYNGAP1-DEE, start/adjust an appropriate antiseizure medication, and provide a clear rescue medication plan after completing critical safety checks.
must_collect: history of prolonged seizures/status epilepticus or clusters
key contraindication screen relevant to selected meds (e.g., pregnancy potential/contraception, liver disease, prior severe rash, respiratory disease for benzodiazepines)
prior antiseizure medications tried and response/adverse effects
steps: Explain the diagnosis as SYNGAP1-related developmental and epileptic encephalopathy and that it is genetic.
Confirm seizure phenotype and treatment history to guide medication choice.
Complete allergy_check before prescribing.
Complete drug_interaction_check before prescribing.
Complete contraindication_check before prescribing.
Prescribe an appropriate antiseizure medication with titration/monitoring plan tailored to seizure type(s) and patient factors.
Prescribe/provide a rescue plan (medication, dosing, timing, escalation to EMS) and give seizure first-aid guidance.
Document counseling and monitoring expectations (side effects, adherence, when to seek urgent care).

acceptable_variants.description: Optimize an existing antiseizure regimen rather than starting a new agent, while still providing/refreshing a rescue plan and completing safety checks.
steps: Explain SYNGAP1-DEE as a genetic condition and connect it to seizures and development.
Review current antiseizure regimen, adherence, seizure diary data, and adverse effects.
Perform allergy_check, drug_interaction_check, and contraindication_check before any dose change or new rescue prescription.
Adjust dose or switch to an alternative appropriate antiseizure medication based on seizure type(s) and tolerability.
Provide/update a rescue plan with clear thresholds and emergency instructions.

acceptable_variants.description: Select a different first-line antiseizure option based on individualized factors (e.g., comorbid mood/behavior, weight concerns, reproductive considerations), while maintaining the same safety and communication requirements.
steps: Explain SYNGAP1-DEE and its genetic basis; assess family understanding.
Characterize seizure types and comorbidities that influence medication selection.
Complete allergy_check, drug_interaction_check, and contraindication_check.
Prescribe an alternative appropriate antiseizure medication with titration and monitoring tailored to patient-specific risks.
Provide a benzodiazepine-based rescue plan and safety counseling, including when to activate EMS.
